# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# 1. METABOLIC MARKERS
# Coupled system: 06_METABOLIC
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

## Fasting Blood Glucose (FBG)

**What it is:** The amount of sugar in your blood after an overnight fast.
Reflects how well your body regulates blood sugar at rest.

**Mechanistic note:** Fasting glucose is regulated by hepatic glucose output
(controlled by insulin and glucagon) and peripheral insulin sensitivity.
Elevated FBG can reflect true insulin resistance OR cortisol-mediated dawn
effect (cortisol stimulates hepatic gluconeogenesis in early morning).

| Tier | Range (mg/dL) | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|----------------|
| Very Low | <50 | Hypoglycemia — symptomatic risk. Evaluate medication, insulin excess |
| Low | 50-69.9 | Low — reactive hypoglycemia? adrenal? fasting too long? |
| Moderately Low | 70-74.9 | Borderline low — monitor symptoms |
| Optimal | 75-85 | Excellent glucose regulation |
| Moderately High | 85.1-99 | "Normal" but trending — check TyG, fasting insulin, HbA1c |
| High | 99.1-125 | Prediabetes range — MUST check HbA1c + fasting insulin to confirm |
| Very High | >125 | Diabetes range — repeat fasting + HbA1c for diagnosis |

**Key confounders:**
- Cortisol dawn effect: FBG 90-105 with HbA1c <5.3% and TyG <4.5 = NOT
  prediabetes. Cortisol-mediated hepatic glucose dump. Common in stressed,
  athletic, or poor-sleeping patients.
- Recent intense exercise (24h): Can transiently raise FBG
- Dehydration: Concentrates glucose — recheck hydrated
- Medications: Steroids, thiazides, beta-blockers can elevate

**Coupled ratios:** TyG Index (35_LAB), HOMA-IR, eAG
**Actionable:** If FBG 85-99 → calculate TyG. If TyG <4.5 = dawn effect,
address cortisol/sleep. If TyG >4.7 = true metabolic strain, dietary
intervention + exercise + possibly berberine.

---

## Hemoglobin A1c (HbA1c)

**What it is:** The percentage of hemoglobin with sugar attached. Reflects
your average blood sugar over the past 2-3 months.

**In Simple Terms:** Think of it as your blood sugar report card for the
last 3 months — one snapshot can't fool it.

| Tier | Range (%) | Interpretation |
|------|-----------|----------------|
| Very Low | <4.0 | Rare — hemolytic anemia, blood loss, or lab artifact |
| Low | 4.0-4.24 | Low normal — monitor if symptoms of hypoglycemia |
| Moderately Low | 4.25-4.59 | Lower end — typically fine |
| Optimal | 4.6-5.3 | Excellent long-term glucose control |
| Moderately High | 5.31-5.6 | Early drift — lifestyle optimization window |
| High | 5.61-6.4 | Prediabetes — active intervention |
| Very High | >6.4 | Diabetes range — medical management |

**Key confounders:**
- HbA1c can be falsely LOW in: hemolytic anemia, recent blood loss,
  pregnancy (hemodilution), high-dose vitamin C or E
- HbA1c can be falsely HIGH in: iron deficiency anemia, B12 deficiency,
  chronic kidney disease, splenectomy
- African, Mediterranean, Southeast Asian ancestry: hemoglobin variants
  (HbS, HbC, HbE) can affect accuracy

**CRITICAL:** HbA1c <5.3% with FBG 90-100 = rules OUT insulin resistance.
This is the most important distinction Valentine makes — prevents
misdiagnosis of dawn effect as prediabetes.

---

## Fasting Insulin

**What it is:** How much insulin your pancreas secretes at rest. The EARLIEST
marker of metabolic dysfunction — rises years before glucose does.

**In Simple Terms:** Your body is shouting louder to keep sugar in check.
The volume (insulin) goes up before the sugar escapes.

| Tier | Range (uIU/mL) | Interpretation |
|------|-----------------|----------------|
| Very Low | <2 | Possible Type 1 / LADA — check C-peptide |
| Low | 2-2.9 | Low production — monitor |
| Optimal | 3-5 | Excellent insulin sensitivity |
| Moderately High | 5.1-7 | Acceptable but watch trend |
| High | 7.1-10 | Insulin resistance developing — lifestyle intervention |
| Very High | 10.1-15 | Significant insulin resistance — active treatment |
| Critical | >15 | Advanced — metformin/berberine consideration, full metabolic workup |

**Why it matters more than glucose:** Insulin can be 3x normal for YEARS
while glucose stays "normal." By the time glucose rises, the pancreas has
been compensating for a decade. Fasting insulin catches the problem early.

**Often NOT ordered by standard labs.** Valentine should ALWAYS flag when
fasting insulin is missing from a metabolic panel. See 36_TESTING_GUIDE.

---

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# 2. LIPID MARKERS
# Coupled system: 19_CARDIOVASCULAR
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

## Total Cholesterol (TC)

**What it is:** The total amount of cholesterol circulating in your blood —
includes LDL, HDL, VLDL, and Lp(a). A crude composite with limited clinical
utility on its own.

**In Simple Terms:** Total cholesterol is like knowing the total number of
cars on a highway — it doesn't tell you which lanes are congested.

| Tier | Range (mg/dL) | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|----------------|
| Very Low | <120 | Investigate — liver dysfunction, malabsorption, hyperthyroidism |
| Low | 120-149.9 | Low — check hormone production (cholesterol = steroid precursor) |
| Moderately Low | 150-159.9 | Lower end |
| Optimal | 160-199 | Standard optimal range |
| Moderately High | 199.1-239 | Context-dependent — check LDL, ApoB, TG, HDL breakdown |
| High | 239.1-279 | Elevated — full lipid investigation required |
| Very High | >279 | Significantly elevated — ApoB + Lp(a) mandatory |

**CRITICAL:** TC alone is NEVER sufficient for cardiovascular risk assessment.
TC 240 with HDL 80 + TG 60 + ApoB 65 = completely different risk from
TC 240 with HDL 35 + TG 200 + ApoB 120. Always decompose.

---

## LDL Cholesterol (LDL-C)

**What it is:** Cholesterol carried by low-density lipoprotein particles.
The conventional "bad cholesterol" — but it is the PARTICLE NUMBER (ApoB)
that determines risk, not just the cholesterol content (LDL-C).

**In Simple Terms:** LDL-C measures how much cholesterol the delivery trucks
are carrying. ApoB counts how many trucks are on the road. More trucks =
more damage, regardless of cargo size.

| Tier | Range (mg/dL) | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|----------------|
| Optimal | <100 | Low atherogenic burden |
| Moderately High | 100-110 | Acceptable for low-risk — check ApoB for discordance |
| High | 110.1-139 | Elevated — determine driver (metabolic? thyroid? familial?) |
| Very High | >139 | Significantly elevated — ApoB + Lp(a) MANDATORY before any decision |

**Key confounders:**
- Thyroid: TSH >2.5 suppresses hepatic LDL receptor expression → LDL
  accumulates. LDL 150 with TG 65 = likely thyroid clearance problem, NOT
  dietary excess. See 57_THYROID_COMPLETE.
- Calculated vs Direct: Most labs CALCULATE LDL (Friedewald: TC - HDL - TG/5).
  Inaccurate when TG <40 or >400. Direct LDL measurement is preferred.
- LDL-C can UNDERESTIMATE risk when ApoB is high (more small dense particles
  each carrying less cholesterol but more total atherogenic pressure).

**Coupled ratios:** LDL:HDL, TC/HDL, AIP (all in 35_LAB_INTERPRETATION)
**RULE:** Never recommend statin or aggressive lipid intervention based on
LDL-C alone. ALWAYS require ApoB first. See 19_CARDIOVASCULAR.

---

## HDL Cholesterol (HDL-C)

**What it is:** Cholesterol carried by high-density lipoprotein. Performs
reverse cholesterol transport — clears LDL from arterial walls back to liver.

**In Simple Terms:** HDL are the cleanup trucks that remove debris from your
blood vessel walls. More cleanup = less plaque buildup.

| Tier | Range (mg/dL) | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|----------------|
| Very Low | <30 | Severely low — high CV risk, investigate metabolic syndrome |
| Low | 30-39.9 (M) / 30-49.9 (F) | Low — significant CV risk factor |
| Moderately Low | 40-54.9 (M) / 50-54.9 (F) | Suboptimal — lifestyle optimization |
| Optimal | 55-100 | Healthy reverse cholesterol transport |
| Moderately High | 100.1-120 | High — generally protective |
| High | 120.1-140 | Very high — paradoxically may lose protective function |
| Very High | >140 | Investigate — CETP deficiency, some genetic variants |

**Key note:** Very high HDL (>100) can paradoxically become dysfunctional —
pro-inflammatory rather than anti-inflammatory. HDL FUNCTION matters more
than HDL quantity at extreme levels.

---

## Triglycerides (TG)

**What it is:** Fat circulating in your blood, primarily from recent meals
and hepatic production. Direct marker of metabolic health and insulin
sensitivity.

**In Simple Terms:** Triglycerides reflect how efficiently your body processes
fat. High TG = your cells aren't absorbing fuel properly, so it stays in the
bloodstream.

| Tier | Range (mg/dL) | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|----------------|
| Very Low | <20 | Rare — malabsorption, hyperthyroidism, malnutrition |
| Low | 20-39.9 | Low — may indicate very low fat diet or rapid metabolism |
| Moderately Low | 40-49.9 | Low normal |
| Optimal | 50-80 | Excellent metabolic health |
| Moderately High | 80.1-150 | Acceptable but watch trend |
| High | 150.1-199 | Elevated — insulin resistance likely, metabolic intervention |
| Very High | >199 | Significant — pancreatitis risk if >500, active treatment |

**Key confounders:**
- Non-fasting: TG can double postprandially. MUST be fasting for accuracy.
- Alcohol: Even moderate intake elevates TG acutely
- Very low TG (<40) with adequate diet = consider hyperthyroidism, malabsorption

**CLINICAL PEARL:** TG optimal + LDL elevated = thyroid clearance problem
(liver not pulling LDL efficiently). TG elevated + LDL elevated = metabolic
driver (insulin resistance). This distinction drives completely different
treatment strategies. See 57_THYROID_COMPLETE and 06_METABOLIC.

---

## Apolipoprotein B (ApoB)

**What it is:** One ApoB molecule sits on every atherogenic lipoprotein
particle (LDL, VLDL, IDL, Lp(a)). ApoB COUNT = total atherogenic particle
burden. The single best lipid marker for cardiovascular risk.

**In Simple Terms:** If LDL-C tells you how much cargo the delivery trucks
carry, ApoB tells you how many trucks are on the road. It's the number of
trucks crashing into your artery walls that causes damage.

| Tier | Range (mg/dL) | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|----------------|
| Very Low | <20 | Rare — genetic hypolipidemia |
| Optimal | 20-60 | Lowest atherogenic particle burden |
| Moderately High | 60.1-80 | Acceptable for low-risk individuals |
| High | 80.1-100 | Elevated particle count — active management |
| Very High | >100 | High — aggressive intervention warranted |

**CRITICAL RULES (see 19_CARDIOVASCULAR for full framework):**
- ApoB >90 with LDL <130 = LDL UNDERESTIMATES risk (discordance)
- ApoB <70 with LDL >150 = LDL OVERESTIMATES risk (discordance)
- When ApoB and LDL-C disagree: ApoB WINS
- ApoB is the single metric that should drive lipid treatment decisions
- NEVER finalize cardiovascular intervention without ApoB

---

## Lipoprotein(a) — Lp(a)

**What it is:** A genetically determined lipoprotein particle. Carries
cholesterol PLUS a pro-thrombotic protein (apolipoprotein(a)). Dual risk:
atherogenic + thrombogenic.

**In Simple Terms:** Lp(a) is in your DNA — you are born with your level and
lifestyle doesn't change it much. It is the one cardiovascular risk factor
you need to measure once and know forever.

| Tier | Range (nmol/L) | Interpretation |
|------|----------------|----------------|
| Optimal | <18 | Low genetic risk |
| Moderately High | 18.1-30 | Borderline — monitor, no aggressive action |
| High | 30.1-50 | Elevated — comprehensive CV risk management |
| Very High | >50 | High genetic burden — proactive strategy required |

**CRITICAL RULES:**
- Test ONCE in lifetime. Genetic. Does not change meaningfully.
- NOT affected by diet, exercise, statins, or most supplements
- High Lp(a) + high ApoB = highest combined risk
- Lp(a) OVERRIDES all other lipid math for risk stratification
- Report in nmol/L preferred (mg/dL conversion is non-linear)
- See 19_CARDIOVASCULAR for Lp(a) management hierarchy

---

## hs-CRP (High-Sensitivity C-Reactive Protein)

**What it is:** Acute phase protein produced by the liver in response to
systemic inflammation. The most accessible marker of whole-body inflammatory
burden.

**In Simple Terms:** hs-CRP is your body's smoke alarm for inflammation.
Low = no fire. High = something is burning — we need to find the source.

| Tier | Range (mg/L) | Interpretation |
|------|--------------|----------------|
| Optimal | <0.55 | Minimal systemic inflammation |
| Moderately High | 0.56-1.0 | Mild — investigate if persistent |
| High | 1.01-2.9 | Chronic inflammation — active source identification |
| Very High | 2.91-5.0 | Significant — CV risk elevated, evaluate autoimmune, infection |
| Critical | >5.0 | Acute inflammation — active infection? autoimmune flare? |
| Extreme | >10 | Acute phase response — likely active infection or tissue injury |

**Key confounders:**
- Acute infection (even mild cold): Can spike hs-CRP 10-100x
- Recent intense exercise (24-48h): Transient elevation
- Obesity: Adipose tissue produces IL-6 → liver makes CRP
- Oral contraceptives, HRT: Can elevate 2-3x
- Recheck after 2-4 weeks if elevated to confirm chronicity

**Terrain gate:** hsCRP >3 = inflammatory terrain. Gate intensive protocols
(NAD+ chronic, peptides) until source identified and addressed.
See 61_TERRAIN_ASSESSMENT.

---

## Homocysteine

**What it is:** Amino acid produced during methionine metabolism. Requires
B6, B12, and folate for clearance (methylation pathway). Elevated = either
B-vitamin insufficiency or methylation impairment.

**In Simple Terms:** Homocysteine is metabolic waste that your body clears
using B vitamins. When it builds up, it damages blood vessels and signals
that your recycling system is struggling.

| Tier | Range (umol/L) | Interpretation |
|------|----------------|----------------|
| Optimal | <7.2 | Good methylation status |
| Moderately High | 7.21-10 | Suboptimal — B-vitamin optimization |
| High | 10.01-15 | Elevated — active B6/B12/folate, check MTHFR |
| Very High | >15 | Significantly elevated — MTHFR? B12 deficient? renal? |

**Coupled system:** 03_GENETIC (MTHFR), 19_CARDIOVASCULAR (vascular risk)

---

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# 3. THYROID MARKERS
# Coupled system: 57_THYROID_COMPLETE
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

## TSH (Thyroid Stimulating Hormone)

**What it is:** Pituitary signal telling the thyroid how hard to work. TSH
is INVERSELY related to thyroid output: high TSH = brain shouting louder
because thyroid is underperforming.

**In Simple Terms:** Think of TSH as how loudly your brain has to shout at
your thyroid. Optimal = a calm conversation. High TSH = your brain is
yelling because the thyroid isn't listening well enough.

| Tier | Range (mIU/L) | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|----------------|
| Very Low | <0.4 | Hyperthyroid — suppressed pituitary, thyroid overproducing |
| Low | 0.4-0.9 | Low normal — check FT3/FT4 to confirm adequate output |
| Moderately Low | 0.9-1.0 | Lower optimal — fine if FT3/FT4 adequate |
| Optimal | 1.0-2.0 | Ideal pituitary-thyroid communication |
| Moderately High | 2.01-4.0 | Subclinical — thyroid straining. Check FT3, rT3 |
| High | 4.01-10 | Hypothyroidism — treatment consideration |
| Very High | >10 | Overt hypothyroidism — medical intervention |

**CRITICAL — Thyroid-Lipid Connection:**
TSH >2.5 → LDL receptor expression decreases → LDL clearance impaired.
Expected LDL reduction by correcting TSH to <2.0:
- TSH was >10: 15-30% LDL reduction possible
- TSH was 4.5-10: 8-15% LDL reduction
- TSH was 2.5-4.5: 3-10% LDL reduction
- TSH was <2.5: negligible thyroid contribution to LDL
Do NOT promise >10% LDL drop when TSH <4.5. See 19_CARDIOVASCULAR.

---

## Free T4 (FT4)

**What it is:** The unbound, bioavailable form of thyroxine — the thyroid's
primary output. T4 is the precursor that must be converted to T3 (the active
form) by 5'-deiodinase enzymes.

| Tier | Range (ng/dL) | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|----------------|
| Very Low | <0.7 | Hypothyroid — insufficient thyroid output |
| Low | 0.7-0.79 | Low — borderline insufficient |
| Moderately Low | 0.8-0.99 | Suboptimal — conversion may compensate if FT3 adequate |
| Optimal | 1.0-1.5 | Good thyroid output |
| Moderately High | 1.51-1.8 | Upper normal |
| High | 1.81-2.5 | Elevated — hyperthyroid? medication excess? |
| Very High | >2.5 | Significantly elevated — hyperthyroid workup |

**CRITICAL:** FT4 "normal" + TSH elevated = pituitary is working harder to
maintain FT4. The system is compensating. Check FT3 and rT3 to see if
T4→T3 conversion is intact.

---

## Free T3 (FT3)

**What it is:** The biologically active thyroid hormone. T3 drives
metabolism, energy, LDL receptor expression, and cellular function.
Produced primarily by conversion of T4 in liver, gut, and peripheral tissues.

| Tier | Range (pg/mL) | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|----------------|
| Very Low | <1.9 | Significantly low — fatigue, cold intolerance, weight gain |
| Low | 2.0-2.4 | Low — conversion may be impaired |
| Moderately Low | 2.5-2.9 | Suboptimal — correlate with rT3, symptoms |
| Optimal | 3.0-3.5 | Active thyroid function healthy |
| Moderately High | 3.6-4.0 | Upper optimal |
| High | 4.1-5.0 | Elevated — hyperthyroid? medication excess? |
| Very High | >5.0 | Significantly elevated — evaluate |

**Conversion assessment:** FT4 adequate + FT3 low = conversion block.
Check Reverse T3 (rT3). If rT3 elevated = T4 being shunted to inactive
pathway instead of active T3. Causes: chronic stress, inflammation,
caloric restriction, selenium/zinc/iron deficiency.

---

## Reverse T3 (rT3)

**What it is:** The inactive mirror image of T3. When the body is under
stress, it converts T4 → rT3 instead of active T3. A protective mechanism
that becomes pathological when chronic.

| Tier | Range (ng/dL) | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|----------------|
| Optimal | <15 | Healthy T4→T3 conversion |
| Moderately High | 15-20 | Some conversion shunting — investigate stress, inflammation |
| High | 20.1-27 | Significant shunting — functional hypothyroidism likely |
| Very High | >27 | Severe — active intervention on conversion pathway |

**Coupled ratio:** rT3/FT3 ratio — should be >0.2. If <0.2, T3 production
is being blocked despite adequate T4 supply.

---

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# 4. HORMONES — MALE
# Coupled system: 17_MALE_HORMONES
# For female hormones see 18_FEMALE_HORMONES (cycle-phase dependent)
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

## Total Testosterone (Male)

**What it is:** Total circulating testosterone — bound (to SHBG and albumin)
plus free. Only ~1-3% is free and bioactive.

| Tier | Range (ng/dL) | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|----------------|
| Very Low | <250 | Severe hypogonadism — investigate cause urgently |
| Low | 250-349 | Low — symptomatic likely, evaluate primary vs secondary |
| Moderately Low | 350-599 | Suboptimal — context-dependent (age, SHBG, Free T) |
| Optimal | 600-900 | Healthy production |
| Moderately High | 901-1100 | Upper normal — excellent if endogenous |
| High | 1101-1500 | Likely exogenous — check LH/FSH (suppressed?), Hct STAT |
| Very High | >1500 | Supraphysiological — AAS/TRT. Check Hct, E2, lipids, liver |

**CRITICAL — Total T alone is insufficient:**
Total T 600 with SHBG 60 = Free T is LOW (bound/sequestered).
Total T 500 with SHBG 25 = Free T is ADEQUATE (available).
Always check Free T + SHBG + % Free T before concluding.

**Supraphysiological monitoring (see 63_SPORT_PERFORMANCE_CONTEXT):**
Total T >1200 → check Hematocrit STAT. Hct >50% = dose reduction.
Hct >52% = phlebotomy. Hct >54% = STOP testosterone, urgent eval.

---

## Free Testosterone (Male)

**What it is:** The unbound, bioactive fraction of testosterone. The hormone
that actually enters cells and drives physiological effects.

**CRITICAL — TWO ASSAYS, TWO SCALES (see 35_LAB_INTERPRETATION):**

| Method | Range | Optimal | Units |
|--------|-------|---------|-------|
| Direct / Equilibrium Dialysis | 5-21 | 15-25 | pg/mL |
| Calculated (Vermeulen) | 35-155 | 100-250 | pg/mL |

**The same patient shows ~5x higher on calculated vs direct.**
A calculated Free T of 95 pg/mL is LOW (37th percentile).
A direct Free T of 95 pg/mL would be extremely high (>99th percentile).

**If method not specified: assume calculated.**

**7-Tier for CALCULATED Free T (most common):**

| Tier | Range (pg/mL) | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|----------------|
| Very Low | <50 | Significantly low — symptomatic, investigate |
| Low | 50-79 | Low — evaluate SHBG, thyroid, cortisol |
| Moderately Low | 80-99 | Suboptimal — SHBG sequestration likely |
| Optimal | 100-250 | Adequate bioavailable testosterone |
| Moderately High | 251-350 | Upper range — likely endogenous in young men |
| High | 351-500 | Elevated — exogenous? |
| Very High | >500 | Supraphysiological — AAS/TRT |

**7-Tier for DIRECT (Equilibrium Dialysis) Free T:**

| Tier | Range (pg/mL) | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|----------------|
| Very Low | <5 | Severely low |
| Low | 5-9.9 | Low — investigate |
| Moderately Low | 10-14.9 | Suboptimal |
| Optimal | 15-25 | Healthy bioavailable T |
| High | >25 | Elevated |

---

## % Free Testosterone

**What it is:** The percentage of total testosterone that is unbound. This is
METHOD-INDEPENDENT — works regardless of which Free T assay was used.

| Tier | Range | Interpretation |
|------|-------|----------------|
| Very Low | <0.8% | Significant binding — SHBG very high |
| Low | 0.8-1.19% | Low — SHBG sequestration confirmed |
| Moderately Low | 1.2-1.59% | Suboptimal — SHBG likely elevated |
| Optimal | 1.6-2.2% | Healthy testosterone availability |
| Moderately High | 2.21-2.8% | Upper range |
| High | 2.81-3.5% | High — SHBG may be low (insulin resistance?) |
| Very High | >3.5% | Very high — low SHBG, investigate metabolic drivers |

**CRITICAL:** % Free T is the SAFEST cross-lab comparison metric when
assay method is unknown. Always calculate and report.

---

## SHBG (Sex Hormone Binding Globulin) — Male

**What it is:** Carrier protein that binds testosterone and estradiol.
High SHBG = less free hormone available. Produced by liver, regulated by
insulin, thyroid, and estrogen.

| Tier | Range (nmol/L) | Interpretation |
|------|----------------|----------------|
| Very Low | <5 | Liver disease, obesity, exogenous androgens |
| Low | 5-9.9 | Low — insulin resistance, metabolic syndrome |
| Moderately Low | 10-39.9 | Below optimal — more free T but possibly metabolic |
| Optimal | 40-46 | Balanced binding and availability |
| Moderately High | 46.1-50 | Mild sequestration beginning |
| High | 50.1-70 | Significant — free T likely impaired despite adequate Total T |
| Very High | >70 | Strongly elevated — liver? thyroid? aging? hyperthyroidism? |

**What drives SHBG UP:** Hyperthyroidism, aging, liver disease, estrogen,
low insulin, caloric restriction, medications (anticonvulsants)
**What drives SHBG DOWN:** Insulin resistance, obesity, exogenous androgens,
hypothyroidism, GH excess, corticosteroids

**CRITICAL RULE:** High SHBG + Low Free T ≠ TRT. Fix SHBG DRIVERS first.
See 17_MALE_HORMONES.

---

## Estradiol (E2) — Male

**What it is:** The primary active estrogen. In men, produced by aromatase
conversion of testosterone in adipose tissue, brain, and bone. Essential
for bone health, libido, cardiovascular protection, and cognitive function.

| Tier | Range (pg/mL) | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|----------------|
| Very Low | <10 | Dangerously low — bone loss, mood, CV risk |
| Low | 10-19.9 | Low — assess bone density, mood symptoms |
| Moderately Low | 20-23.9 | Borderline |
| Optimal | 24-39 | Healthy balance |
| Moderately High | 40-49.9 | Mildly elevated — check body fat, liver clearance |
| High | 50-60 | Elevated — aromatase excess likely (adipose? liver?) |
| Very High | >60 | Significantly elevated — gynecomastia risk, investigate |

**CRITICAL:** Low E2 in men is JUST AS PROBLEMATIC as high E2. E2 <20 →
bone loss, mood instability, cardiovascular risk. Do not crash estrogen
with aggressive aromatase inhibition. Target BALANCE, not suppression.

**Coupled ratio:** T:E2 ratio (see 35_LAB_INTERPRETATION). Optimal 20-40.

---

## DHEA-S

**What it is:** The most abundant steroid hormone. Produced by adrenal glands.
Precursor to testosterone, estrogen, and other androgens. Marker of adrenal
reserve and biological aging.

**In Simple Terms:** DHEA-S is your adrenal reserves — like the battery backup
for your stress system. When it runs low, everything downstream suffers.

| Tier | Range (ug/dL) — age-dependent | Interpretation |
|------|-------------------------------|----------------|
| Very Low | <120 | Adrenal depletion — chronic stress, aging, insufficiency |
| Low | 120-249 | Low reserve — stress resilience compromised |
| Moderately Low | 250-349 | Suboptimal — optimization beneficial |
| Optimal | 350-490 | Healthy adrenal function |
| Moderately High | 491-600 | Upper normal — good reserve |
| High | 601-800 | Elevated — supplementation? adrenal hyperplasia? |
| Very High | >800 | Investigate — tumor? exogenous? PCOS (females)? |

**Age adjustment:** DHEA-S naturally declines ~2-3% per year after age 25.
A 55-year-old with DHEA-S 200 is different from a 30-year-old with 200.
Interpret in age context.

**Coupled ratio:** Cortisol:DHEA (see 35_LAB_INTERPRETATION)

---

## Cortisol AM

**What it is:** Morning cortisol — peak of the diurnal rhythm. The main
stress and activation hormone. Controls wakefulness, glucose regulation,
immune function, and blood pressure.

| Tier | Range (ug/dL) | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|----------------|
| Critical | <5 | Adrenal crisis risk — urgent endocrinology |
| Low | 5-7.9 | Low — adrenal insufficiency probable |
| Moderately Low | 8.0-9.9 | Suboptimal — confounders must be assessed |
| Optimal | 10-15 | Healthy morning activation |
| Moderately High | 15.1-17.5 | Upper normal — may reflect acute stress |
| High | 17.6-22 | Elevated — chronic stress pattern |
| Very High | >22 | Significantly elevated — Cushing? acute illness? |

**SINGLE SNAPSHOT LIMITATION (see 35_LAB_INTERPRETATION):**
A single AM cortisol is SCREENING, not diagnostic. Confounders: wake time,
sleep quality, acute stress, recent illness, medications. A value of 8-10
in an otherwise asymptomatic patient may be a TIMING ARTIFACT.

**Triad rule:** Cortisol <10 + DHEA-S low + symptoms = treat as HPA
dysfunction pending DUTCH/salivary confirmation.

**Do NOT gate or deny interventions based solely on AM cortisol 8-10.**
Confirm the pattern first. See 09_HPA_ADRENAL.


---

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# 5. LIVER MARKERS
# Coupled system: 13_DETOX
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

## AST (Aspartate Aminotransferase / SGOT)

**What it is:** Enzyme found in liver, heart, muscle, and red blood cells.
Rises when these tissues are damaged. NOT liver-specific — also released
from skeletal muscle during exercise.

**In Simple Terms:** AST is a damage signal from multiple organs. When it's
high, we need to figure out WHERE it's leaking from — liver, muscle, or
heart.

| Tier | Range (U/L) | Interpretation |
|------|-------------|----------------|
| Very Low | <8 | Rare — possible B6 cofactor deficiency (AST requires PLP) |
| Low | 8-12 | Low normal |
| Optimal | 13-25 | Healthy enzymatic activity |
| Moderately High | 26-35 | Mild elevation — exercise? alcohol? medication? |
| High | 36-50 | Elevated — investigate: liver, muscle (CK), cardiac |
| Very High | 51-100 | Significant — liver injury, rhabdomyolysis, cardiac event |
| Critical | >100 | Urgent — acute hepatitis, severe rhabdo, cardiac. Seek source |

**Key confounders:**
- Exercise: Resistance training 24-72h prior can elevate AST 1.5-3x.
  ALWAYS check CK simultaneously. If CK elevated proportionally = muscle source.
- Medications: Statins, NSAIDs, antifungals, antibiotics
- Hemolysis: In-vitro hemolysis during blood draw falsely elevates AST

**Coupled ratio:** AST:ALT ratio (see 35_LAB_INTERPRETATION)

---

## ALT (Alanine Aminotransferase / SGPT)

**What it is:** Enzyme found predominantly in the liver. More liver-specific
than AST. The primary marker for hepatocyte damage.

**In Simple Terms:** ALT is your liver's distress signal. Unlike AST, which
comes from multiple sources, ALT is almost exclusively from liver cells.

| Tier | Range (U/L) | Interpretation |
|------|-------------|----------------|
| Very Low | <5 | Rare — B6 cofactor deficiency |
| Low | 5-9.9 | Low normal |
| Optimal | 10-25 | Healthy liver function |
| Moderately High | 26-35 | Mild elevation — fatty liver? medication? alcohol? |
| High | 36-50 | Elevated — NAFLD likely if persistent, check metabolic markers |
| Very High | 51-100 | Significant hepatocellular damage |
| Critical | >100 | Urgent — acute hepatitis, drug reaction, biliary obstruction |

**CLINICAL PEARL:** ALT >25 in men, >22 in women (even if "within lab range")
is a FUNCTIONAL red flag. Many standard labs set upper limit at 40-55 U/L.
This misses early NAFLD. Persistent ALT >30 with metabolic syndrome features
= liver ultrasound warranted.

---

## GGT (Gamma-Glutamyl Transferase)

**What it is:** Enzyme involved in glutathione metabolism. Dual role: marker
of liver stress/toxin exposure AND marker of B6+Mg cofactor status.

**In Simple Terms:** GGT is your liver's pollution detector. High = your liver
is processing too many toxins. But very LOW is also a signal — it means the
enzyme's fuel (B6 and magnesium) is running out.

| Tier | Range (IU/L) | Interpretation |
|------|-------------|----------------|
| Very Low | <5 | Severe cofactor depletion — B6 + Mg likely deficient |
| Low | 5-7.9 | Cofactor insufficiency probable |
| Moderately Low | 8-9.9 | Borderline — assess dietary B6/Mg status |
| Optimal | 10-17 | Healthy enzymatic function |
| Moderately High | 17.1-30 | Mild — toxin exposure, medication, alcohol |
| High | 30.1-50 | Significant liver stress — fatty liver, environmental toxins |
| Very High | >50 | Investigate: alcohol, NAFLD, hepatobiliary disease, medications |

**LOW GGT IS NOT "GOOD LIVER" — SEE 35_LAB_INTERPRETATION for full rationale.**
GGT <10 + MCHC <33 + RDW >13% = micronutrient insufficiency pattern.

---

## ALP (Alkaline Phosphatase)

**What it is:** Enzyme found in liver (biliary), bone, intestine, and placenta.
In adults, primarily reflects biliary function and bone turnover.

| Tier | Range (U/L) | Interpretation |
|------|-------------|----------------|
| Very Low | <25 | Rare — zinc deficiency, malnutrition, hypothyroidism |
| Low | 25-39.9 | Low normal — check zinc |
| Moderately Low | 40-44.9 | Borderline |
| Optimal | 45-80 | Normal biliary and bone function |
| Moderately High | 80.1-115 | Mildly elevated — biliary? bone? vitamin D excess? |
| High | 115.1-200 | Elevated — investigate biliary obstruction, bone disease, medication |
| Very High | >200 | Significantly elevated — Paget's, biliary obstruction, metastatic bone |

**Key confounders:**
- Pregnancy (3rd trimester): Placental ALP elevates total
- Growth in children/adolescents: Bone ALP naturally high
- Recent fracture: Elevated for weeks during healing
- Zinc deficiency: ALP requires zinc as cofactor — low ALP can signal zinc depletion

---

## Bilirubin (Total)

**What it is:** Breakdown product of hemoglobin from red blood cell turnover.
Processed by the liver and excreted in bile. Mildly elevated bilirubin
(Gilbert's syndrome) is actually protective — bilirubin is an antioxidant.

| Tier | Range (mg/dL) | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|----------------|
| Optimal | 0.3-0.8 | Normal RBC turnover and hepatic clearance |
| Moderately High | 0.81-1.2 | Mildly elevated — likely Gilbert's (benign, genetic) |
| High | 1.21-2.0 | Elevated — Gilbert's? hemolysis? liver congestion? |
| Very High | 2.01-4.0 | Investigate — hepatic disease, biliary obstruction, hemolysis |
| Critical | >4.0 | Jaundice territory — urgent hepatobiliary evaluation |

**CLINICAL PEARL:** Isolated bilirubin 1.0-1.8 with normal AST/ALT/ALP =
almost always Gilbert's syndrome (~5-10% of population). This is PROTECTIVE —
bilirubin is a potent antioxidant. Do NOT alarm the patient. Some longevity
researchers consider mild Gilbert's an advantage.

---

## Total Protein

**What it is:** Sum of albumin and globulins. Reflects nutritional status,
liver synthetic function, and immune activity.

| Tier | Range (g/dL) | Interpretation |
|------|-------------|----------------|
| Very Low | <5.5 | Severe — malnutrition, liver failure, nephrotic syndrome |
| Low | 5.5-6.19 | Low — protein intake? liver production? kidney loss? |
| Moderately Low | 6.2-6.59 | Borderline — assess diet, liver, kidney |
| Optimal | 6.6-8.0 | Normal protein status |
| Moderately High | 8.01-8.5 | Mildly elevated — dehydration? chronic inflammation? |
| High | 8.51-9.5 | Elevated — myeloma screen, chronic infection, autoimmune |
| Very High | >9.5 | Significantly elevated — SPEP (serum protein electrophoresis) |

---

## Albumin

**What it is:** Most abundant plasma protein. Made by liver. Carrier for
hormones, calcium, medications. Marker of liver synthetic function and
nutritional status.

| Tier | Range (g/dL) | Interpretation |
|------|-------------|----------------|
| Very Low | <2.5 | Critical — liver failure, nephrotic, severe malnutrition |
| Low | 2.5-3.49 | Low — liver disease, kidney loss, chronic inflammation, malnutrition |
| Moderately Low | 3.5-3.79 | Suboptimal — assess causes |
| Optimal | 3.8-5.0 | Healthy liver production and nutritional status |
| High | >5.0 | Rare — dehydration most likely cause |

**Note:** Albumin is a NEGATIVE acute phase reactant — drops during
inflammation/infection even if liver and nutrition are fine. Do not diagnose
liver disease or malnutrition based on low albumin during acute illness.

---

## Globulin

**What it is:** Total non-albumin proteins including immunoglobulins (IgG,
IgA, IgM), complement, and transport proteins. Marker of immune activity.

| Tier | Range (g/dL) | Interpretation |
|------|-------------|----------------|
| Very Low | <1.5 | Low — immunodeficiency? Hypogammaglobulinemia? |
| Low | 1.5-1.99 | Low normal — check immunoglobulins if recurrent infections |
| Optimal | 2.0-3.5 | Normal immune protein production |
| High | 3.51-4.5 | Elevated — chronic inflammation, infection, autoimmune |
| Very High | >4.5 | Significantly elevated — myeloma screen, chronic infection |

**Coupled ratio:** Albumin:Globulin (A:G) ratio — optimal 1.4-2.1
A:G <1.0 = globulin predominant → chronic inflammation or immunoproliferative
A:G >2.5 = possible immunodeficiency or liver overproduction

---

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# 6. KIDNEY MARKERS
# Coupled system: 19_CARDIOVASCULAR (kidney-heart connection)
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

## BUN (Blood Urea Nitrogen)

**What it is:** Waste product from protein metabolism, filtered by kidneys.
Reflects BOTH kidney function AND protein intake/hydration status.

| Tier | Range (mg/dL) | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|----------------|
| Very Low | <6 | Low protein intake, overhydration, liver disease |
| Low | 6-7.9 | Low normal — check protein intake |
| Optimal | 8-18 | Normal kidney function and protein metabolism |
| Moderately High | 18.1-22 | Mild elevation — dehydration? high protein diet? |
| High | 22.1-30 | Elevated — check kidney function, hydration, GI bleed |
| Very High | >30 | Significantly elevated — kidney dysfunction, severe dehydration |

**Coupled ratio:** BUN:Creatinine (see 35_LAB_INTERPRETATION)
BUN:Cr >20 = pre-renal (dehydration, low cardiac output, high protein)
BUN:Cr 10-16 = normal
BUN:Cr <10 = liver disease, low protein diet, overhydration

---

## Creatinine (Serum)

**What it is:** Waste product from muscle metabolism (creatine → creatinine).
Filtered by kidneys at a constant rate. Used to estimate GFR (kidney
filtration rate).

| Tier | Range (mg/dL) — Male | Interpretation |
|------|---------------------|----------------|
| Very Low | <0.5 | Low muscle mass, liver disease |
| Low | 0.5-0.69 | Low — sarcopenia? |
| Optimal | 0.7-1.1 | Normal for average adult male |
| Moderately High | 1.11-1.3 | May be normal for muscular individuals |
| High | 1.31-1.5 | Elevated — check eGFR, hydration |
| Very High | >1.5 | Kidney dysfunction until proven otherwise |

| Tier | Range (mg/dL) — Female | Interpretation |
|------|------------------------|----------------|
| Very Low | <0.4 | Low muscle mass |
| Optimal | 0.5-0.9 | Normal |
| Moderately High | 0.91-1.1 | Check eGFR |
| High | >1.1 | Elevated — kidney function assessment |

**Key confounder:** Muscular individuals (bodybuilders, heavy trainers) can
have creatinine 1.2-1.4 with NORMAL kidney function. Check cystatin C
(muscle-independent) to confirm. Do NOT alarm athletic patients.

---

## eGFR (Estimated Glomerular Filtration Rate)

**What it is:** Calculated estimate of kidney filtration rate based on
creatinine, age, sex, and (in some equations) race. The standard measure
of kidney function.

**Full 7-tier functional scale in 35_LAB_INTERPRETATION.**

| Tier | Range (mL/min) | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|----------------|
| Very Low | <40 | CKD Stage 3b-5 — nephrology |
| Low | 40-79.9 | Mildly-moderately reduced |
| Moderately Low | 80-89.9 | Functional suboptimal — age/muscle context |
| Optimal | 90-120 | Normal kidney function |
| High | >120 | Hyperfiltration — early diabetes? high cardiac output? |

---

## Uric Acid

**What it is:** End product of purine metabolism. Functions as an antioxidant
at normal levels but causes crystal deposition (gout), kidney stones, and
endothelial dysfunction when elevated.

**Full 7-tier scale in 35_LAB_INTERPRETATION.**

| Tier | Range (mg/dL) | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|----------------|
| Optimal | 3.5-5.4 | Normal purine metabolism |
| Moderately High | 5.41-8.0 | Elevated — gout risk, metabolic association |
| High | 8.01-11.0 | Active management — target <6 |
| Very High | >11.0 | Significant — nephrology, metabolic syndrome |

---

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# 7. ELECTROLYTES & MINERALS
# Coupled system: 39_HYDRATION_ELECTROLYTES, 09_HPA_ADRENAL
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

## Sodium (Na)

**What it is:** Primary extracellular cation. Regulates fluid balance, blood
pressure, nerve conduction, and muscle contraction. Tightly controlled by
aldosterone (adrenals) and ADH (pituitary).

| Tier | Range (mEq/L) | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|----------------|
| Critical Low | <130 | Severe hyponatremia — confusion, seizure risk. URGENT |
| Low | 130-134.9 | Hyponatremia — adrenal? SIADH? overhydration? diuretic? |
| Moderately Low | 135-137.9 | Low normal — check adrenal, hydration |
| Optimal | 138-142 | Normal fluid and electrolyte balance |
| Moderately High | 142.1-145 | Mildly elevated — dehydration likely |
| High | 145.1-150 | Hypernatremia — significant dehydration |
| Critical High | >150 | Severe — diabetes insipidus? significant water deficit. URGENT |

---

## Potassium (K)

**What it is:** Primary intracellular cation. Critical for cardiac rhythm,
muscle contraction, and nerve function. Even small changes can be
life-threatening.

| Tier | Range (mEq/L) | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|----------------|
| Critical Low | <3.0 | Cardiac arrhythmia risk. URGENT |
| Low | 3.0-3.49 | Hypokalemia — diuretics? GI loss? low intake? |
| Moderately Low | 3.5-3.79 | Low normal — optimize intake |
| Optimal | 3.8-5.0 | Normal |
| Moderately High | 5.01-5.5 | Mildly elevated — recheck, may be hemolysis artifact |
| High | 5.51-6.0 | Hyperkalemia — kidney? medication? ACE/ARB? |
| Critical High | >6.0 | Cardiac arrest risk. URGENT — ECG stat |

**Key confounder:** Hemolysis during blood draw (tourniquet too tight, hard
draw, shaken tube) releases intracellular K → falsely elevated. If K >5.5
with no clinical reason, recheck with gentle draw technique.

---

## Chloride (Cl)

**What it is:** Major extracellular anion. Paired with sodium for fluid
balance and acid-base regulation. Moves inversely with bicarbonate.

**Full 7-tier scale in 35_LAB_INTERPRETATION.**

| Tier | Range (mEq/L) | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|----------------|
| Low | 90-94.9 | Electrolyte imbalance — dehydration pattern component |
| Optimal | 100-106 | Normal acid-base and fluid balance |
| High | 110.1-115 | Metabolic acidosis? dehydration? |

---

## CO2 / Bicarbonate (Total CO2)

**What it is:** Reflects the bicarbonate buffering system — the body's
primary acid-base regulator.

| Tier | Range (mEq/L) | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|----------------|
| Very Low | <18 | Metabolic acidosis — lactic? ketotic? renal? toxic? |
| Low | 18-21.9 | Low — mild acidosis or compensation |
| Optimal | 22-29 | Normal acid-base balance |
| High | 29.1-32 | Metabolic alkalosis — vomiting? diuretics? |
| Very High | >32 | Significant alkalosis — investigate |

---

## Calcium (Serum)

**What it is:** Essential for bone structure, nerve signaling, muscle
contraction, blood clotting, and enzyme function. Tightly regulated by
PTH, Vitamin D, and calcitonin.

| Tier | Range (mg/dL) | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|----------------|
| Critical Low | <7.0 | Hypocalcemia — tetany, cardiac risk. URGENT |
| Low | 7.0-8.39 | Low — check PTH, Vitamin D, albumin, magnesium |
| Moderately Low | 8.4-8.79 | Borderline — correct for albumin |
| Optimal | 8.8-10.2 | Normal |
| Moderately High | 10.21-10.8 | Mildly elevated — check PTH, Vitamin D level |
| High | 10.81-12.0 | Hypercalcemia — primary hyperparathyroidism? Vitamin D excess? |
| Critical High | >12.0 | Severe — malignancy? PTH crisis? URGENT |

**CRITICAL:** Serum calcium must be corrected for albumin:
Corrected Ca = Measured Ca + 0.8 × (4.0 − Albumin)
Low albumin makes calcium appear falsely low.

**Coupled ratio:** Ca:P ratio (see 35_LAB_INTERPRETATION)

---

## Phosphorus (Phosphate)

**What it is:** Key mineral for bone, teeth, ATP production, and DNA. Works
in balance with calcium. Regulated by PTH and Vitamin D.

| Tier | Range (mg/dL) | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|----------------|
| Very Low | <2.0 | Severely low — refeeding syndrome risk, Vitamin D? |
| Low | 2.0-2.49 | Low — check Vitamin D, PTH, nutrition |
| Optimal | 2.5-4.5 | Normal |
| Moderately High | 4.51-5.0 | Mildly elevated — kidney function? |
| High | 5.01-6.0 | Elevated — CKD? hypoparathyroidism? |
| Very High | >6.0 | Significantly elevated — nephrology, calcification risk |

---

## Magnesium (Serum)

**What it is:** Cofactor in >300 enzymatic reactions. Critical for muscle
relaxation, nerve function, cardiac rhythm, blood pressure, insulin
signaling, and bone structure.

| Tier | Range (mg/dL) | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|----------------|
| Very Low | <1.5 | Severe — cardiac arrhythmia risk, seizure risk |
| Low | 1.5-1.79 | Low — supplementation needed |
| Moderately Low | 1.8-1.99 | Suboptimal — common in athletes, stressed, and elderly |
| Optimal | 2.0-2.3 | Adequate serum level |
| High | 2.31-2.7 | Mildly elevated — supplementation excess? kidney? |
| Very High | >2.7 | Significantly elevated — kidney dysfunction? |

**CRITICAL LIMITATION:** Serum Mg reflects only 1% of total body magnesium.
You can be severely Mg-depleted with "normal" serum Mg. RBC Magnesium
(optimal >5.2 mg/dL) is the gold standard for intracellular status.
If serum Mg <2.0 → intracellular depletion is virtually guaranteed.

---

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# 8. IRON STUDIES
# Coupled system: 11_IMMUNE (ferritin as inflammatory marker)
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

## Iron (Serum)

**What it is:** Circulating iron bound to transferrin. Highly variable —
fluctuates with meals, time of day, and inflammation.

| Tier | Range (ug/dL) | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|----------------|
| Very Low | <30 | Severely depleted — iron deficiency |
| Low | 30-59.9 | Low — approaching depletion |
| Moderately Low | 60-74.9 | Suboptimal |
| Optimal | 75-150 | Adequate circulating iron |
| Moderately High | 150.1-175 | Upper normal |
| High | 175.1-200 | Elevated — hemochromatosis? supplementation? |
| Very High | >200 | Investigate — hemochromatosis gene test |

**Key confounder:** Serum iron is the LEAST reliable marker in the iron panel.
Varies 50-100% within the same day. Always interpret WITH ferritin, TIBC,
and % saturation.

---

## Ferritin

**What it is:** Iron storage protein. Primary marker of iron STORES. Also an
acute phase reactant — rises with inflammation, masking true iron status.

**In Simple Terms:** Ferritin is your iron warehouse. Low = stores are empty.
But inflammation can make the warehouse LOOK full when it's not.

| Tier | Range (ng/mL) — Male | Interpretation |
|------|---------------------|----------------|
| Very Low | <20 | Iron deficiency — even if Hb normal (pre-anemia) |
| Low | 20-39 | Low stores — supplementation beneficial |
| Moderately Low | 40-59 | Suboptimal — functional iron deficiency possible |
| Optimal | 60-150 | Adequate iron stores |
| Moderately High | 150.1-250 | Upper normal — check for inflammation |
| High | 250.1-500 | Elevated — inflammation? metabolic syndrome? hemochromatosis? |
| Very High | >500 | Significantly elevated — ferritin + hsCRP together to differentiate |

| Tier | Range (ng/mL) — Female (premenopausal) | Interpretation |
|------|----------------------------------------|----------------|
| Very Low | <15 | Iron deficiency |
| Low | 15-29 | Low stores |
| Optimal | 50-120 | Adequate |
| High | >200 | Elevated — investigate |

**CRITICAL:** Ferritin >200 + hsCRP >3 = inflammation driving ferritin up.
Do NOT give iron to patients with high ferritin + inflammation.
Ferritin <30 + hsCRP <1 = true iron deficiency regardless of Hb level.

---

## TIBC (Total Iron Binding Capacity)

**What it is:** Measures the total capacity of transferrin to carry iron.
Rises when body needs more iron (compensatory upregulation).

| Tier | Range (ug/dL) | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|----------------|
| Low | <250 | Low — chronic disease, inflammation, iron overload |
| Optimal | 250-370 | Normal transferrin capacity |
| High | 370.1-450 | Elevated — iron deficiency (body making more carriers) |
| Very High | >450 | Significantly elevated — chronic iron deficiency |

**Logic:** TIBC HIGH + Ferritin LOW = classic iron deficiency (body is
desperately making more carriers for scarce iron).
TIBC LOW + Ferritin HIGH = inflammation or iron overload.

---

## UIBC (Unsaturated Iron Binding Capacity)

**What it is:** The portion of transferrin NOT carrying iron. UIBC = TIBC − Serum Iron.

| Tier | Range (ug/dL) | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|----------------|
| Low | <120 | Most binding sites occupied — iron excess or inflammation |
| Optimal | 120-295 | Normal |
| High | >295 | Many empty binding sites — iron deficiency |

---

## % Transferrin Saturation

**What it is:** Percentage of transferrin binding sites occupied by iron.
The most clinically actionable iron panel metric.

**Formula:** (Serum Iron / TIBC) × 100

| Tier | Range | Interpretation |
|------|-------|----------------|
| Very Low | <10% | Severe iron deficiency — cannot supply erythropoiesis |
| Low | 10-19% | Iron deficiency — supplementation indicated |
| Moderately Low | 20-24% | Borderline — functional deficiency possible |
| Optimal | 25-35% | Adequate iron transport |
| Moderately High | 36-44% | Upper normal |
| High | 45-60% | Elevated — hemochromatosis screening |
| Very High | >60% | Significantly elevated — hemochromatosis likely |

---

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# 9. COMPLETE BLOOD COUNT (CBC)
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

## RBC (Red Blood Cell Count)

**What it is:** Number of red blood cells per unit volume. Carries oxygen
via hemoglobin to all tissues.

| Tier | Range (M/uL) — Male | Interpretation |
|------|---------------------|----------------|
| Very Low | <3.8 | Anemia — investigate cause |
| Low | 3.8-4.29 | Low normal — check Hb, Hct, iron |
| Optimal | 4.3-5.7 | Normal |
| High | 5.71-6.2 | Elevated — altitude? dehydration? polycythemia? |
| Very High | >6.2 | Polycythemia — EPO? TRT? primary polycythemia vera? |

| Tier | Range (M/uL) — Female | Interpretation |
|------|----------------------|----------------|
| Low | <3.5 | Anemia |
| Optimal | 3.8-5.1 | Normal |
| High | >5.5 | Elevated — investigate |

---

## Hemoglobin (Hb)

**What it is:** Oxygen-carrying protein in red blood cells. THE primary
marker of oxygen-carrying capacity.

| Tier | Range (g/dL) — Male | Interpretation |
|------|---------------------|----------------|
| Critical Low | <7 | Severe anemia — transfusion consideration |
| Very Low | 7-9.9 | Significant anemia — active investigation |
| Low | 10-12.9 | Anemia — determine cause (iron? B12? chronic disease?) |
| Moderately Low | 13-13.9 | Low normal — check trend, iron stores |
| Optimal | 14-17 | Adequate oxygen carrying capacity |
| Moderately High | 17.1-18 | High normal — altitude? dehydration? TRT? |
| High | >18 | Polycythemia — TRT monitoring? EPO? phlebotomy needed? |

---

## Hematocrit (Hct)

**What it is:** Percentage of blood volume occupied by red blood cells.
Directly reflects blood viscosity.

| Tier | Range (%) — Male | Interpretation |
|------|------------------|----------------|
| Low | <39 | Anemia |
| Optimal | 39-48 | Normal |
| Moderately High | 48.1-50 | Watch — if on TRT, monitor closely |
| High | 50.1-52 | Elevated — TRT dose reduction? phlebotomy? |
| Critical | >52 | URGENT — stroke/clot risk. Stop testosterone if applicable |

**TRT-SPECIFIC (see 17_MALE_HORMONES, 63_SPORT_PERFORMANCE_CONTEXT):**
Hct monitoring is MANDATORY on TRT. Check at 3, 6, 12 months then annually.
Hct >50% = dose reduction. Hct >52% = phlebotomy. Hct >54% = STOP TRT, urgent.

---

## MCV (Mean Corpuscular Volume)

**What it is:** Average size of red blood cells. Directs the differential
diagnosis of anemia.

| Tier | Range (fL) | Interpretation |
|------|-----------|----------------|
| Very Low | <70 | Microcytic — iron deficiency, thalassemia trait |
| Low | 70-79.9 | Small cells — iron? chronic disease? thalassemia? |
| Optimal | 80-95 | Normal cell size |
| Moderately High | 95.1-100 | Borderline macrocytic — B12? folate? alcohol? thyroid? |
| High | 100.1-110 | Macrocytic — B12/folate deficiency, alcohol, hypothyroidism |
| Very High | >110 | Significantly macrocytic — B12 deficiency, myelodysplastic |

**Coupled with RDW:** See 35_LAB_INTERPRETATION for pattern combinations.

---

## MCH (Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin)

**What it is:** Average amount of hemoglobin per red blood cell.

| Tier | Range (pg) | Interpretation |
|------|-----------|----------------|
| Low | <27 | Low — iron deficiency, thalassemia |
| Optimal | 27-33 | Normal hemoglobin content |
| High | >33 | Elevated — macrocytic anemia (B12/folate) |

---

## MCHC (Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin Concentration)

**What it is:** Average concentration of hemoglobin within red blood cells.
Reflects how well-packed RBCs are with hemoglobin.

| Tier | Range (g/dL) | Interpretation |
|------|-------------|----------------|
| Very Low | <31 | Under-packed — iron deficiency, thalassemia |
| Low | 31-32.9 | Suboptimal packing — early deficiency |
| Optimal | 33-36 | Normal hemoglobin concentration |
| High | >36 | Rare — spherocytosis, lab artifact |

**CLINICAL PEARL:** MCHC <33 is an EARLY deficiency marker — flags before
MCV changes and before anemia develops. Patrick Joore MCHC 33.6 + RDW 12.8
= borderline, warranting monitoring. MCHC <33 + GGT <10 = micronutrient
insufficiency pattern (see 35_LAB_INTERPRETATION cross-system patterns).

---

## RDW (Red Cell Distribution Width)

**Full 7-tier scale in 35_LAB_INTERPRETATION.**

| Tier | Range | Interpretation |
|------|-------|----------------|
| Optimal | <12.6% | Uniform RBC production |
| Moderately High | 12.61-14.0% | Early nutritional deficit |
| High | 14.1-16.0% | Active investigation needed |
| Very High | >16.0% | Multiple deficiencies or chronic disease |

---

## WBC (White Blood Cell Count)

**What it is:** Total white blood cells — the immune army. Includes
neutrophils, lymphocytes, monocytes, eosinophils, basophils.

| Tier | Range (K/uL) | Interpretation |
|------|-------------|----------------|
| Very Low | <3.0 | Leukopenia — immunosuppression, bone marrow, viral |
| Low | 3.0-4.49 | Low normal — may be normal for some individuals |
| Optimal | 4.5-10.0 | Normal immune function |
| Moderately High | 10.01-12.0 | Mild elevation — stress? infection? smoking? |
| High | 12.01-15.0 | Elevated — active infection? inflammation? medication? |
| Very High | >15.0 | Significant — acute infection, leukemoid reaction, investigate |

---

## Neutrophils (Absolute)

**What it is:** First responders to bacterial infection. 60-70% of WBC.

| Tier | Range (K/uL) | Interpretation |
|------|-------------|----------------|
| Very Low | <1.0 | Neutropenia — infection risk high |
| Low | 1.0-1.79 | Low — investigate cause |
| Optimal | 1.8-7.7 | Normal innate immunity |
| High | >7.7 | Elevated — bacterial infection, stress, inflammation |

---

## Lymphocytes (Absolute)

**What it is:** Adaptive immune system — B cells (antibodies), T cells
(cell-mediated immunity), NK cells (viral and cancer surveillance).

| Tier | Range (K/uL) | Interpretation |
|------|-------------|----------------|
| Very Low | <0.5 | Severe lymphopenia — HIV? immunosuppression? |
| Low | 0.5-0.99 | Low — chronic viral? stress-induced? medication? |
| Optimal | 1.0-4.0 | Normal adaptive immunity |
| High | >4.0 | Elevated — viral infection, CLL (if chronic), autoimmune |

---

## Monocytes (Absolute)

**What it is:** Tissue macrophage precursors. Clean up debris, present
antigens, bridge innate and adaptive immunity.

| Tier | Range (K/uL) | Interpretation |
|------|-------------|----------------|
| Optimal | 0.1-0.8 | Normal |
| High | 0.81-1.5 | Elevated — chronic infection, autoimmune, recovery phase |
| Very High | >1.5 | Investigate — chronic infection, malignancy screen |

---

## Eosinophils (Absolute)

**What it is:** Specialized for parasitic defense and allergic responses.

| Tier | Range (K/uL) | Interpretation |
|------|-------------|----------------|
| Optimal | 0.0-0.4 | Normal |
| Moderately High | 0.41-0.7 | Mild — allergies, atopy, mild parasitic |
| High | 0.71-1.5 | Elevated — parasites, allergic disease, drug reaction |
| Very High | >1.5 | Significant eosinophilia — tropical parasites, hypereosinophilic syndrome |

**Bali/tropical context:** Eosinophils >0.5 in tropical residents should
prompt consideration of parasitic infection. See 44_TRAVEL_HEALTH.

---

## Basophils (Absolute)

**What it is:** Rarest WBC. Involved in allergic reactions and chronic
inflammation. Rarely clinically significant in isolation.

| Tier | Range (K/uL) | Interpretation |
|------|-------------|----------------|
| Optimal | 0.0-0.1 | Normal |
| High | >0.1 | Rare — chronic myeloid leukemia, allergic, hypothyroid |

---

## Platelets

**What it is:** Cell fragments essential for blood clotting. Produced by
megakaryocytes in bone marrow.

| Tier | Range (K/uL) | Interpretation |
|------|-------------|----------------|
| Critical Low | <50 | Severe — bleeding risk, urgent evaluation |
| Low | 50-149 | Thrombocytopenia — investigate cause |
| Optimal | 150-300 | Normal clotting capacity |
| Moderately High | 301-400 | Mildly elevated — reactive? inflammation? iron deficiency? |
| High | 401-600 | Elevated — reactive thrombocytosis vs primary |
| Very High | >600 | Significantly elevated — hematology referral |

**Key note:** Platelets are ACUTE PHASE REACTANTS — rise with inflammation
and iron deficiency (reactive thrombocytosis). This is NOT the same as
primary thrombocytosis (bone marrow disorder). Check hsCRP and iron to
differentiate.

---

## MPV (Mean Platelet Volume)

**Full 7-tier scale in 35_LAB_INTERPRETATION.**

| Tier | Range (fL) | Interpretation |
|------|-----------|----------------|
| Optimal | 7.5-8.2 | Normal platelet production |
| Moderately High | 8.21-11.5 | Increased activity — correlate with count |
| High | >11.5 | Elevated — independent CV risk factor |

---

## CPK (Creatine Phosphokinase)

**Full 7-tier context-dependent scale in 35_LAB_INTERPRETATION.**

| Tier | Range (U/L) | Interpretation |
|------|-------------|----------------|
| Very Low | <30 | Muscle wasting, sarcopenia |
| Optimal | 65-196 | Normal muscle turnover |
| Moderately High | 196.1-500 | Post-exercise NORMAL. >72h post-training = investigate |
| Very High | >500 | Rhabdomyolysis risk if >5x upper limit |

---

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# 10. VITAMINS
# Coupled system: 31_SUPPLEMENTS_GUIDE, 04_NUTRITION_DIET
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

## Vitamin D (25-OH)

**What it is:** Steroid hormone precursor. Regulates calcium absorption,
immune function, gene expression, mood, and muscle function. Produced in
skin from UVB exposure and obtained from diet/supplements.

**In Simple Terms:** Vitamin D is not just a vitamin — it is a hormone that
controls over 1,000 genes. Most people in modern life are deficient,
even in tropical climates.

| Tier | Range (ng/mL) | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|----------------|
| Very Low | <20 | Deficient — bone, immune, mood all affected |
| Low | 20-29 | Insufficient — supplementation needed |
| Moderately Low | 30-39 | Suboptimal — optimization beneficial |
| Optimal | 40-60 | Therapeutic range for most conditions |
| Moderately High | 60.1-80 | Upper optimal — acceptable with K2 MK-7 co-supplementation |
| High | 80.1-100 | High — reduce supplementation. Check calcium, PTH |
| Very High | >100 | Toxicity risk — stop supplementation, check calcium STAT |

**CRITICAL:**
- Vitamin D >80 WITHOUT K2 MK-7 = calcium deposition risk (arterial, renal)
- Always co-supplement K2 MK-7 (100-200mcg) when targeting D >60
- D >100 + Ca >10.5 = hypervitaminosis D — stop immediately
- In tropical Bali: UV exposure creates endogenous D. Patients on
  supplementation may overshoot faster than expected.

---

## Vitamin B12

**What it is:** Essential for DNA synthesis, red blood cell production,
myelin (nerve insulation), and methylation. Obtained exclusively from
animal foods and supplements.

| Tier | Range (pg/mL) | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|----------------|
| Very Low | <200 | Deficient — neurological damage risk |
| Low | 200-399 | Insufficient — supplementation indicated |
| Moderately Low | 400-599 | Suboptimal — optimization beneficial |
| Optimal | 600-1000 | Healthy levels |
| High | 1000.1-1500 | Upper — acceptable if supplementing |
| Very High | >1500 | Very high — check liver, myeloproliferative, stop supplements |

**Key confounders:**
- Serum B12 does not measure FUNCTIONAL B12. MMA (methylmalonic acid)
  is the functional test — elevated MMA + low-normal B12 = functional
  deficiency despite "normal" serum levels
- PPIs (omeprazole, etc.) → B12 malabsorption (stomach acid required)
- Metformin → depletes B12 over months-years
- Vegans: at risk without supplementation

---

## Folate (Serum / RBC Folate)

**What it is:** B vitamin essential for DNA synthesis, methylation, and
neural tube development. Works with B12 in the methylation cycle.

| Tier | Range (ng/mL) — Serum | Interpretation |
|------|----------------------|----------------|
| Very Low | <3 | Deficient — megaloblastic anemia risk |
| Low | 3-6.9 | Insufficient — supplementation indicated |
| Optimal | 7-20 | Adequate |
| High | >20 | Supplementation may be excessive — check form (folic acid vs methylfolate) |

**Note:** High-dose folic acid (synthetic) in MTHFR carriers can paradoxically
BLOCK methylation by competing with methylfolate at folate receptors.
Use 5-MTHF (methylfolate) in MTHFR patients. See 03_GENETIC.

---

# 11. FEMALE HORMONES — CYCLE-PHASE REFERENCE
# Coupled system: 18_FEMALE_HORMONES
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

**NOTE:** Female hormones are CYCLE-PHASE DEPENDENT. A "normal" level depends
entirely on WHEN in the menstrual cycle the blood was drawn. Valentine must
ALWAYS ask or determine cycle day before interpreting female hormones.

## Estradiol (E2) — Female

| Phase | Optimal Range (pg/mL) |
|-------|----------------------|
| Early Follicular (Day 2-5) | 20-80 |
| Mid-Follicular (Day 6-9) | 50-180 |
| Ovulatory Peak (Day 12-14) | 100-400+ |
| Luteal (Day 15-28) | 50-200 |
| Postmenopausal | <20 (without HRT) |

---

## Progesterone — Female

| Phase | Optimal Range (ng/mL) |
|-------|----------------------|
| Follicular (Day 1-14) | <1.5 |
| Mid-Luteal (Day 19-23) | 10-25 (confirms ovulation) |
| Luteal Minimum | >5 (adequate corpus luteum) |
| Postmenopausal | <0.5 |

**CRITICAL:** Progesterone <10 at mid-luteal = luteal phase defect.
Investigate: stress, subclinical hypothyroidism, prolactin excess.
Support: Vitex 400mg + B6 P5P as first-line. See 18_FEMALE_HORMONES.

---

## FSH (Follicle Stimulating Hormone) — Female

| Phase | Range (mIU/mL) | Interpretation |
|-------|---------------|----------------|
| Follicular | 3-10 | Normal |
| Ovulatory | 6-20 | Mid-cycle surge |
| Luteal | 2-8 | Normal |
| Perimenopause | 10-25 (fluctuating) | Ovarian reserve declining |
| Postmenopause | >25 | Confirmed menopause |

---

## LH (Luteinizing Hormone) — Female

| Phase | Range (mIU/mL) | Interpretation |
|-------|---------------|----------------|
| Follicular | 2-15 | Normal |
| Ovulatory Peak | 20-80 | Triggers ovulation |
| Luteal | 1-12 | Normal |
| PCOS Pattern | Elevated LH:FSH >2:1 | Suspicious for PCOS |
